title: From bulk to interface dynamics, in and out of equilibrium
authors: Lila Sarfati, Julien Tailleur, Frédéric van Wijland
abstract: We study the dynamics of weakly deformed interfaces separating two stable phases, starting from the fluctuating hydrodynamics of the phase-separating fields. Using a well-chosen definition for the interface and the dynamical-action formalism to represent path probabilities, we derive the linear relaxation of the interface and the fluctuations around it for a large class of models. Our method applies to equilibrium dynamics, where it recovers and complements existing results, but also extends to their non-equilibrium counterparts. We explain how non-linear terms can be systematically computed and illustrate their derivations in the case of (active) model A. We highlight the danger of a popular ansatz used to derive interface dynamics, which was rigorously established in equilibrium but is uncontrolled for active field theories.
summary: This paper establishes a rigorous field-theoretic method to derive the effective motion of an interface from the underlying bulk dynamics. It corrects widespread errors in applying mean-field approximations to active matter and provides a systematic way to compute non-linear interface evolution.
main_problem: The lack of a controlled, systematic field-theoretical framework to derive interface dynamics from bulk dynamics, particularly for non-equilibrium and active systems where common mean-field approximations are mathematically unjustified.
main_result: The authors derive a unified framework that recovers known equilibrium results (like the Edwards-Wilkinson equation) and extends to non-equilibrium models, demonstrating that a popular mean-field ansatz is uncontrolled for active theories and can lead to incorrect predictions.
method: The study uses the dynamical-action formalism (Janssen-De Dominicis action) and a geometric projection operator to integrate out bulk fluctuations, employing perturbation theory and spectral analysis of the linearized operator.
model_or_system: The paper focuses on interfaces separating two stable phases in scalar field theories, specifically examining Model A (non-conserved) and Model B (conserved) dynamics, as well as active versions of these models.
key_observables: Interface height field h(r, t), interface relaxation rate (dispersion relation), drift velocity, and bulk fluctuation field chi.
important_parameters: Temperature (or noise amplitude) T, surface tension sigma, interface width xi, and wavevector q.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a low-temperature/weak-noise limit, a large-wavelength limit (q xi << 1), and focuses on weakly deformed interfaces.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a cartoon of a fluctuating interface with bulk deformations; Figure 2 shows relaxation dynamics; Figure 4 shows the exponential decay of Fourier amplitudes of excited modes.
paper_structure: The paper begins by identifying the methodological gap and criticizing the mean-field ansatz, then introduces a new geometric definition for the interface, proceeds to derive the linear dynamics for Model A and Model B, and concludes by discussing non-linear terms and active matter applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Fluctuations of the interface between two sta- ble phases around its flat average. The interface fluctu- ations result from both a shift of the mean-field profile mc (dark blue) and the fluctuations around it.
Figure 2. (Left) Relaxation time of a one-dimensional interface in a two-dimensional model B from an initial condition 0.1
Figure 3. Relaxation time of a one-dimensional inter- face in a 512 × 512 system from an initial condition 0.1
Figure 4. Relaxation of a one-dimensional interface in a two-dimensional model B, starting from an initial con- dition 0.1
